Schopenhauer despised Hegel and deliberately scheduled his Berlin lectures at the same hour as Hegel's, a rivalry he lost and resented for life.
Feuerbach studied under Hegel in Berlin for two years before turning into one of the most influential critics of Hegelian philosophy.
Cicero attacked Mark Antony in his Philippics, and Antony had him proscribed and executed in 43 BCE.
Cicero was a longtime political supporter of Pompey and sided with him against Caesar in the civil war.
Cicero and Brutus were friends and correspondents; Cicero dedicated his treatises Brutus and Orator to him and supported the Liberators' cause after Caesar's assassination.
Cicero championed the young Octavian against Mark Antony in the Senate, but Octavian later consented to Cicero's proscription and death.
Liszt championed Wagner's music, conducted the premiere of Lohengrin while Wagner was in exile, and became his father-in-law when Wagner married Liszt's daughter Cosima.
King Ludwig II of Bavaria rescued Wagner from debt, funded his operas and the Bayreuth Festival Theatre, and made his later career possible.
The young Nietzsche was an intimate of Wagner's household and wrote The Birth of Tragedy under his influence before their friendship ended in a famous public break.
Wagner and Brahms headed the opposing camps of the War of the Romantics, the central aesthetic rivalry of 19th-century German music.
Richard served his elder brother Edward IV as a loyal lieutenant and northern governor, then seized the throne from Edward's son after the king's death.
Richard III, as Lord Protector, deposed his nephew Edward V and confined him in the Tower of London, where the boy king disappeared.
Margaret Beaufort conspired against Richard III to place her son Henry Tudor on the throne, and Richard attainted her for her role in Buckingham's rebellion.
Virgil and Horace were close friends in the literary circle of Maecenas, to whom Virgil personally introduced Horace.
John XXIII was directly succeeded as pope by Paul VI, who carried the Second Vatican Council he had opened to completion.
Giovanni Montini, the future Paul VI, worked for years as Pius XII's closest aide and substitute secretary of state in the Vatican.
Cnut the Great drove Olaf II from the Norwegian throne in 1028, and Olaf was killed at Stiklestad in 1030 trying to reclaim it from Cnut's supporters.
Harold Harefoot was Cnut's son and seized the English throne after his father's death, displacing Cnut's intended heir Harthacnut.
Pius XII was directly succeeded as pope by John XXIII in 1958.
Xerxes I was the son and chosen successor of Darius the Great and inherited his father's project of invading Greece.
Artaxerxes I was the son of Xerxes I and took the Persian throne after his father's assassination in 465 BCE.
Leonidas I and his Spartans made their famous last stand at Thermopylae against the invading army of Xerxes I in 480 BCE.
Themistocles engineered the Greek naval victory at Salamis that destroyed Xerxes I's invasion fleet in 480 BCE.
Nicholas II was the son of Alexander III and succeeded him as the last emperor of Russia in 1894.
Nicholas II and Alexandra Feodorovna were devoted husband and wife who ruled Russia together and were executed together by the Bolsheviks in 1918.
Rasputin became the trusted spiritual adviser and healer of Nicholas II's household, and his influence over the tsar's family helped discredit the monarchy.
Wilhelm II and Nicholas II were royal cousins whose personal Willy-Nicky telegrams failed to avert the First World War, in which their empires fought each other.
Locke worked with Robert Boyle at Oxford, absorbed his experimental empiricism, and posthumously edited Boyle's General History of the Air.
John Wilkes Booth, a Confederate sympathizer, assassinated President Abraham Lincoln at Ford's Theatre in 1865.
Louis XIV sponsored Moliere's troupe, stood godfather to his son, and protected him against the campaign to ban Tartuffe.
Moliere and Lully created the comedie-ballet genre together in works like Le Bourgeois gentilhomme before their partnership ended in rivalry.
La Fontaine and Moliere were friends in the celebrated Parisian literary circle of the 1660s that also included Racine and Boileau.
Ada Lovelace worked with Charles Babbage on his Analytical Engine, and her published notes on it contain what is regarded as the first computer program.
Ada Lovelace was Lord Byron's only legitimate child; her mother raised her on mathematics to counter the poet's influence after he abandoned the family.
William III and Mary II were married and ruled England jointly as co-monarchs after the Glorious Revolution of 1688.
William III invaded England in 1688 and deposed James II, his uncle and father-in-law, then defeated his attempt at restoration at the Battle of the Boyne.
Antonio Salieri taught the young Schubert composition and counterpoint in Vienna for about five years.
Pope Innocent III gave Francis of Assisi verbal approval of his rule in 1210, allowing the founding of the Franciscan order.
As Cardinal Ugolino, the future Gregory IX was the official protector of the Franciscan order and a personal friend of Francis, whom he canonized as pope in 1228.
Anthony of Padua joined Francis's order, and Francis personally authorized him to teach theology to the Franciscan friars.
James II was the second son of Charles I, whose execution in 1649 drove the young James into continental exile.
Mary II was James II's eldest daughter and accepted the English crown with her husband after her father was deposed in the Glorious Revolution.
Eugenio Pacelli served Pius XI as cardinal secretary of state for nearly a decade and directly succeeded him as Pope Pius XII in 1939.
Sulla advanced the young Pompey's career, granted him a triumph, and gave him the surname Magnus for his victories in Sulla's cause.
Crassus and Pompey, despite mutual jealousy, shared two consulships and joined Caesar in the First Triumvirate that dominated Roman politics.
Pompey decisively defeated Mithridates VI in the Third Mithridatic War, driving him to his death and annexing his kingdom.
Miltiades, once a vassal tyrant under Darius the Great, commanded the Athenian army that defeated Darius's invasion force at Marathon in 490 BCE.
Daniel Bernoulli and Euler were friends and collaborators at the St. Petersburg Academy, working together on hydrodynamics and mechanics.
Frederick the Great employed Euler at the Berlin Academy for twenty-five years, though the king's disdain eventually drove Euler back to Russia.
Catherine the Great recalled Euler to the St. Petersburg Academy in 1766 on generous terms and supported him for the rest of his career.
Euler guided the young Lagrange through correspondence on the calculus of variations, held back his own results so Lagrange would get credit, and arranged his succession at the Berlin Academy.
According to tradition, Saint George was a soldier in Diocletian's army who was tortured and executed in 303 for refusing to renounce Christianity during the emperor's Great Persecution.
Gregory XVI was directly succeeded as pope by Pius IX in 1846.
Victor Emmanuel II annexed the Papal States and seized Rome in 1870, and the excommunicating Pius IX declared himself a prisoner in the Vatican.
Garibaldi defended the 1849 Roman Republic that drove Pius IX into exile and remained the pope's bitter opponent throughout Italian unification.
Napoleon III's troops restored Pius IX to Rome in 1849 and the French garrison protected papal rule there until 1870.
Goethe worked personally with the young Schopenhauer on color theory in Weimar, leading to Schopenhauer's treatise On Vision and Colors.
Francis I and Charles V fought four wars in their lifelong contest for Italy and European supremacy, with Charles even holding Francis captive after the Battle of Pavia.
Charles V was Joanna of Castile's son and ruled Castile in her name while keeping his mother confined at Tordesillas as nominal co-monarch.
Charles V was Ferdinand II of Aragon's grandson and inherited the Spanish crowns on his death, uniting Castile and Aragon under one ruler.
Charles V entrusted the Habsburg Austrian lands to his brother Ferdinand and abdicated the imperial title to him in 1556, dividing the dynasty into Spanish and Austrian branches.
Philip II was Charles V's son and received Spain, the Netherlands, and the overseas empire when his father abdicated in 1556.
Charles V made Titian his court painter and knighted him, sitting for portraits that defined the emperor's image.
Clement VII allied against Charles V in the League of Cognac, and the emperor's unpaid troops sacked Rome in 1527 and held the pope prisoner.
Flavius Aetius, who had known the Huns as a boy hostage, led the Roman-Visigothic army that halted Attila at the Catalaunian Plains in 451.
Pope Leo I met the invading Attila in person near Mantua in 452 and persuaded him to withdraw from Italy without sacking Rome.
Charles VI's Pragmatic Sanction secured the Habsburg succession for his daughter Maria Theresa, who inherited his lands in 1740.
Francis I and Maria Theresa were husband and wife; she secured him the imperial crown while ruling the Habsburg lands herself and bearing their sixteen children.
Frederick the Great seized Silesia from Maria Theresa in 1740, and she fought him through the War of the Austrian Succession and the Seven Years' War to recover it.
Joseph II was Maria Theresa's son and co-regent from 1765, and their frequent clashes over reform defined the final years of her rule.
Maria Theresa married her daughter Marie Antoinette to the future Louis XVI to seal the Franco-Austrian alliance and admonished her by letter for the rest of her life.
Joseph II was Marie Antoinette's eldest brother and traveled to Paris in 1777 to counsel her and Louis XVI, famously helping salvage the royal marriage.
Louis XVII was Marie Antoinette's son; his forced separation from her in the Temple prison and his death in captivity became emblems of the Revolution's severity.
Marie Therese was Marie Antoinette's eldest child and the only member of the imprisoned royal family to survive the Revolution.
Orhan was Osman I's son and succeeded him as the second ruler of the Ottoman state, capturing Bursa as his father lay dying.
Antoninus Pius adopted Marcus Aurelius as his heir, groomed him for two decades, and was directly succeeded by him as emperor in 161.
Hadrian favored the boy Marcus Aurelius, nicknaming him Verissimus, and secured his future succession by requiring Antoninus Pius to adopt him.
Marcus Aurelius made his adoptive brother Lucius Verus co-emperor in Rome's first joint reign, sharing power until Verus's death in 169.
Galen served as court physician to Marcus Aurelius, who kept him in Rome to attend the imperial household during the Antonine plague.
Robespierre relentlessly denounced Lafayette as a traitor to the Revolution, while Lafayette sought to crush the Jacobins, a feud that ended with Lafayette's flight from France in 1792.
Rimsky-Korsakov privately taught Stravinsky composition and orchestration from 1905 until his death in 1908.
Debussy and Stravinsky were mutually admiring friends who played the four-hand piano reduction of The Rite of Spring together before its premiere.
Picasso designed the sets and costumes for Stravinsky's ballet Pulcinella for the Ballets Russes in 1920, and the two remained friends.
Cocteau wrote the libretto for Stravinsky's opera-oratorio Oedipus Rex, premiered in 1927.
Ravel and Stravinsky were close friends in the 1910s and worked together orchestrating Mussorgsky's Khovanshchina for Diaghilev.
Mahler and Richard Strauss maintained a decades-long friendship and correspondence, each conducting and championing the other's works.
Brahms admired Mahler's conducting and his endorsement helped Mahler secure the directorship of the Vienna Court Opera.
Nefertiti was Akhenaten's Great Royal Wife and appears beside him as co-promoter of the revolutionary cult of the Aten.
Ezra Pound championed the unknown Eliot, got Prufrock published, and edited The Waste Land so decisively that Eliot dedicated it to him as il miglior fabbro.
Virginia Woolf and T. S. Eliot were close literary friends, and her Hogarth Press hand-printed his Poems and the first English book edition of The Waste Land.
Bertrand Russell taught Eliot at Harvard and later sheltered and supported the young Eliot and his wife Vivienne in London.
Botticelli worked under Medici patronage in Lorenzo the Magnificent's Florence, painting the Primavera and Birth of Venus for the family circle.
Sixtus IV summoned Botticelli to Rome in 1481 to paint three of the wall frescoes of the newly built Sistine Chapel.
Botticelli became a follower of Savonarola, whose preaching turned the painter toward austere religious subjects in his late career.
Mommsen examined Max Weber's doctoral dissertation and publicly declared the young scholar the one worthy to take the spear from his hand.
Karl Jaspers belonged to Weber's Heidelberg circle and regarded Weber as his decisive intellectual model, writing influential essays on him after his death.
Claudius adopted Nero as his heir and was directly succeeded by him as emperor in 54, allegedly after being poisoned by Nero's mother Agrippina.
Seneca tutored the young Nero and guided his early reign as chief adviser, until Nero forced his old teacher to commit suicide in 65.
According to early Christian tradition, Peter was crucified in Rome during Nero's persecution of Christians after the Great Fire of 64.
According to early Christian tradition, Paul was beheaded in Rome under Nero during the persecution that followed the Great Fire of 64.
Munch and Strindberg were close companions in the Berlin bohemian circle Zum schwarzen Ferkel in the 1890s, and Munch painted Strindberg's portrait.
Garibaldi conquered Sicily and Naples with his Thousand and handed them to Victor Emmanuel II at Teano in 1860, making him king of a united Italy.
Alexandre Dumas accompanied Garibaldi's Expedition of the Thousand, edited and published his memoirs, and was rewarded with a post in liberated Naples.
Francis II succeeded his father Leopold II as Holy Roman Emperor on Leopold's sudden death in 1792.
Napoleon defeated Francis II repeatedly, forced him to dissolve the Holy Roman Empire in 1806, and extracted his daughter's hand in marriage.
Francis relied on Metternich as foreign minister and chancellor, and together they shaped the conservative European order of the Congress of Vienna.
Francis II married his daughter Marie Louise to Napoleon in 1810 to buy peace for Austria, then reclaimed her and her son after Napoleon's fall.
Ferdinand I was Francis II's son and succeeded him as Emperor of Austria in 1835 despite his incapacity, ruling through Metternich's regency council.
Edward IV deposed the Lancastrian Henry VI twice during the Wars of the Roses and had him put to death in the Tower of London in 1471.
Catherine of Valois was Henry VI's mother, and her secret remarriage to Owen Tudor produced the half-brothers Henry VI later ennobled, founding the Tudor line.
Henry VI was crowned king of France in Paris in direct rivalry with Charles VII, who ultimately drove the English from France in the Hundred Years' War.
Velazquez served Philip IV as court painter for nearly four decades, and the king knighted him with the Order of Santiago.
Innocent X sat for Velazquez's celebrated 1650 portrait, pronounced it troppo vero, and rewarded the painter with a gold chain.
Madame de Pompadour was Louis XV's official chief mistress and remained his closest confidante and a power at Versailles for two decades.
William IV was George III's third son, sent by his father into the Royal Navy at thirteen before unexpectedly inheriting the throne.
William IV succeeded his elder brother George IV as king of the United Kingdom in 1830.
William IV was succeeded by his niece Victoria, having lived just long enough for her to turn eighteen and avoid a regency he distrusted.
Edward V was Edward IV's son and heir, whose brief unconsummated reign after his father's sudden death ended in the Tower of London.
According to Herodotus, Cyrus the Great invaded the lands of the Massagetae and was defeated and killed by the army of their queen Tomyris.
Catherine was Peter the Great's second wife; he crowned her empress consort, and she succeeded him as ruling Empress of Russia on his death.
Muhammad Iqbal's letters persuaded Jinnah to return from London to lead the Muslim League, and Jinnah honored him as the spiritual guide of the Pakistan idea.
Augustus banished Ovid to remote Tomis on the Black Sea in 8 CE for what the poet called a poem and a mistake, an exile never lifted.
Leucippus taught Democritus and together they founded the atomist school, with Democritus elaborating his teacher's atomic theory.
Mies van der Rohe and Le Corbusier both trained in Peter Behrens's office, and Mies gave Le Corbusier the prime commissions of his 1927 Weissenhof estate that showcased the new architecture.
Victor Emmanuel III assumed the title Emperor of Ethiopia after Italy's 1936 conquest, usurping the exiled Haile Selassie's throne until 1941.
Alexandre Dumas fils was the illegitimate son of Alexandre Dumas, and father and son became the twin literary celebrities of 19th-century Paris.
Delacroix and Dumas were friends in the Romantic circles of Paris, and Dumas memorialized the painter in lectures and writings after his death.
Jesus called James, son of Zebedee, from his fishing nets to be an apostle and included him in the inner circle present at the Transfiguration.
James and John were brothers, the sons of Zebedee whom Jesus nicknamed the sons of thunder, called together from their father's fishing boat.
Herod Agrippa had James, son of Zebedee, put to the sword around 44 CE, making him the first of the twelve apostles to be martyred.
Claudius banished Seneca to Corsica for eight years, and after the emperor's death Seneca mocked him in the satire Apocolocyntosis.

Petain, once de Gaulle's military mentor, headed Vichy France while de Gaulle led the Free French; Petain's regime condemned de Gaulle to death, and de Gaulle later commuted his old chief's own death sentence.
Adenauer and de Gaulle sealed Franco-German reconciliation with the 1963 Elysee Treaty, founding the postwar Paris-Bonn partnership.
Eisenhower worked with de Gaulle as Allied supreme commander, agreeing to let Free French forces liberate Paris in 1944, and the two later dealt as presidents.
Chekhov befriended and encouraged the young Gorky, and resigned his honorary Academy membership in protest when the tsar annulled Gorky's election.
Benedict XV, who had made Achille Ratti a nuncio and cardinal, was directly succeeded by him as Pope Pius XI in 1922.
Ho Chi Minh trained in Stalin's Comintern in Moscow, and Stalin granted his Democratic Republic of Vietnam diplomatic recognition and support in 1950.
Catherine de' Medici summoned Nostradamus to the French court, consulted him on her children's destinies, and had him appointed counselor and physician-in-ordinary to the king.
Guyuk was Ogedei Khan's eldest son and succeeded him as Great Khan of the Mongol Empire after a five-year regency.
Ogedei dispatched his nephew Batu to conquer the western steppes and Europe, and Ogedei's death in 1241 forced Batu to halt the Mongol advance into Europe.
Catherine was Peter III's wife and seized his throne in a 1762 palace coup, after which the deposed emperor died in custody.
Catherine the Great bought Diderot's library while leaving it in his keeping with a salary, and hosted him in St. Petersburg for months of daily philosophical conversations.
Frederick the Great promoted young Sophie of Anhalt-Zerbst as bride for the Russian heir, and as monarchs he and Catherine allied in the First Partition of Poland.
Petrarch and Boccaccio maintained a devoted twenty-five-year friendship and correspondence that laid the foundations of Renaissance humanism.
Solon succeeded Draco as lawgiver of Athens, repealing Draco's notoriously harsh code except for its homicide law.
The aged Solon publicly warned Athens against his kinsman Peisistratos and opposed the tyranny Peisistratos established over the city.
Fatimah was the daughter of Khadija and Muhammad, and through her the Prophet's line continued after all his sons died young.
Vespucci helped provision Columbus's voyages in Seville, and Columbus wrote in 1505 that Vespucci had always been his friend and wished him well.
Ferdinand II of Aragon made Vespucci the first pilot major of Castile in 1508, charged with training pilots and keeping the master chart of the new discoveries.
Eleanor of Aquitaine was Richard I's mother, ruled England as regent during his crusade, and personally negotiated his ransom from captivity.
Richard I and Philip II led the Third Crusade together but became bitter enemies, fighting a prolonged war over Richard's French territories until Richard's death.
The 1948 Tito-Stalin split expelled Yugoslavia from the Cominform, and Stalin repeatedly plotted to assassinate Tito.
Churchill switched Allied support to Tito's Partisans in 1943 and met him personally in Naples in 1944 to coordinate the war effort.
Khrushchev traveled to Belgrade in 1955 to reconcile with Tito, ending the Soviet-Yugoslav split with the Belgrade Declaration.
Fatimah was the mother of Hasan ibn Ali, through whom the Prophet Muhammad's lineage and the Shia imamate descended.
Fatimah was the mother of Husayn ibn Ali, whose martyrdom at Karbala as the Prophet's grandson defines Shia Islam.
Murad II held the young Vlad hostage at the Ottoman court for years and in 1448 installed him on the Wallachian throne with Ottoman backing.
Anaxagoras was Pericles' teacher and closest intellectual adviser, and Pericles defended him when he was tried for impiety in Athens.
Aspasia was Pericles' lifelong companion and the mother of his son; their union was the most famous partnership in classical Athens.
Pericles put his friend Phidias in charge of the Acropolis building program, commissioning the Parthenon sculptures and the statue of Athena.
Anne Boleyn displaced Catherine of Aragon as Henry VIII's queen, and the struggle over Catherine's annulment triggered the English Reformation.
Albert was Victoria's husband and closest political adviser; his death in 1861 sent her into decades of mourning that defined her reign.
Victoria helped raise her granddaughter Alix of Hesse after her mother's death, and through her Victoria's hemophilia gene passed to the Russian imperial family.
Septimius Severus was Geta's father and raised him to co-emperor in 209, hoping his sons would rule jointly after his death.
Brothers Caracalla and Geta ruled as hostile co-emperors after their father's death until Caracalla had Geta murdered in their mother's arms in 211.
Caligula lived under Tiberius's watch on Capri as his adopted grandson and directly succeeded him as Roman emperor in 37.
Caligula's uncle Claudius was proclaimed emperor by the Praetorian Guard immediately after Caligula's assassination in 41.
Herod Agrippa was a close personal friend of Caligula, who freed him from Tiberius's prison and made him king of Judean territories.
Sun Yat-sen was Chiang's political patron and mentor, appointing him commandant of the Whampoa Military Academy; Chiang succeeded him as leader of the Kuomintang.
Chiang Kai-shek and Mao Zedong fought the decades-long Chinese Civil War, which ended with Mao's communists taking the mainland and Chiang retreating to Taiwan in 1949.
Roosevelt backed Chiang's China as a key wartime ally against Japan and met him at the 1943 Cairo Conference to plan the postwar order in Asia.
Septimius Severus was Caracalla's father, made him co-emperor as a boy, and on his deathbed advised him to enrich the soldiers and scorn everyone else.
Macrinus, Caracalla's praetorian prefect, arranged his assassination in 217 and directly succeeded him as Roman emperor.
Eleanor was FDR's wife and political partner, acting as his eyes and ears across the country and transforming the role of First Lady during his four terms.
Theodore Roosevelt was Eleanor's uncle and gave her away at her 1905 wedding to Franklin, anchoring her in the family's political tradition.
Truman appointed Eleanor Roosevelt to the US delegation to the United Nations, where she chaired the commission that drafted the Universal Declaration of Human Rights.
Kelvin's 1845 letter suggesting an experiment on light and magnetism led Faraday directly to discover the magneto-optic Faraday effect, and the two exchanged scientific ideas for decades.
Lavoisier and Laplace built an ice calorimeter together and co-authored the 1783 'Memoire sur la chaleur', founding the science of calorimetry.
Franklin and Lavoisier served together on the 1784 French royal commission that investigated and debunked Mesmer's animal magnetism.
Washington took the young Lafayette under his wing as a surrogate son and protege, commanding him through the Revolutionary War from Valley Forge to Yorktown.
Renoir and Cezanne were close friends from the Impressionist circle who repeatedly stayed and painted together in Provence in the 1880s.
Pius X was the direct predecessor of Benedict XV, who was elected pope weeks after Pius X's death at the outbreak of the First World War.
In the traditional papal succession Anacletus was the direct predecessor of Clement I, the fourth bishop of Rome.
Early Christian tradition holds that Clement knew the apostles and was personally ordained by Peter, whose Roman church he later led.
Benedict XV personally consecrated Eugenio Pacelli bishop in 1917 and sent him as nuncio to Bavaria, launching the diplomatic career that carried him to the papacy as Pius XII.
Julian was Constantine the Great's nephew and the last emperor of his dynasty, famous for trying to reverse his uncle's Christianization of the empire.
Gregory of Nazianzus studied alongside Julian in Athens and later wrote his famous invectives against Julian's pagan revival and his ban on Christian teachers.
Amenhotep III was Akhenaten's father and immediate predecessor as pharaoh, whose traditional solar court Akhenaten transformed into the revolutionary Aten cult.
Stalin and Roosevelt were two of the Big Three Allied leaders of WWII, meeting at Tehran and Yalta to coordinate the war against Germany and design the postwar order.
Theodore Roosevelt was FDR's fifth cousin and political role model; FDR married Theodore's niece Eleanor and consciously followed his path to the presidency.
Truman was FDR's fourth-term vice president and succeeded to the presidency on Roosevelt's death in April 1945, inheriting the war's endgame and the atomic bomb decision.
Roosevelt and Hitler led opposing sides of the Second World War in Europe, with Hitler declaring war on the United States in December 1941.
Franklin stayed as Hume's houseguest in Edinburgh and the two maintained a warm friendship and scientific-philosophical correspondence.
As American envoy Franklin negotiated the 1778 Treaty of Alliance with Louis XVI, whose military support proved decisive for American independence.
Nerva adopted the general Trajan as his heir to secure the army's loyalty and was directly succeeded by him, beginning the era of the adoptive 'Five Good Emperors'.
Trajan was Hadrian's guardian and kinsman who advanced his career; Hadrian succeeded him as emperor in 117 amid claims of a deathbed adoption.
After Saladin captured Jerusalem, Frederick Barbarossa sent him a formal declaration of war and led the Third Crusade against him, drowning on the march in 1190.
Hurrem Sultan was Selim II's mother and worked within the palace to steer the Ottoman succession toward her own sons.
Nurbanu Sultan was Selim II's favorite and legal wife, wielding great influence at his court and founding the era of the 'Sultanate of Women' as valide sultan.
Selim II was the father and direct predecessor of Murad III, who succeeded him as Ottoman Sultan in 1574.
Selim II commissioned Mimar Sinan to build the Selimiye Mosque in Edirne, which Sinan himself regarded as his masterpiece.
Joseph II admired and consciously imitated Frederick the Great, met him twice, and then fought him in the War of the Bavarian Succession as they competed for leadership in Germany.
Schumann hailed the twenty-year-old Brahms as music's coming genius in his 1853 article 'Neue Bahnen' and launched his career by introducing him to publishers.
Clara Schumann and Brahms shared a passionate, lifelong friendship from Robert's collapse until her death, exchanging letters and championing each other's music for over forty years.
Brahms publicly signed the 1860 manifesto against the New German School led by Liszt, cementing their positions on opposite sides of the War of the Romantics.
Brahms discovered Dvorak through the Austrian State Stipendium jury, recommended him to his publisher Simrock, and guided his rise to international fame.
Wells and Shaw were friendly antagonists for half a century, most famously when Wells tried to seize control of the Fabian Society from Shaw's old guard and was outmaneuvered.
Hobbes wrote the hostile Third Set of Objections to Descartes' Meditations, and the two philosophers carried on an acrimonious dispute over materialism and the foundations of knowledge.
Hobbes tutored the exiled future Charles II in mathematics in Paris, and as king Charles protected his old teacher with a pension when Leviathan drew charges of atheism.
Hobbes and Boyle fought a famous public controversy in the 1660s over Boyle's air-pump experiments and the existence of the vacuum, a defining dispute about experimental science.
Engels led the Marxist campaign against Bakunin's anarchist faction in the First International, culminating in Bakunin's expulsion at the 1872 Hague Congress.
Hitler's Condor Legion helped Franco win the Spanish Civil War, and the two dictators famously negotiated Spain's wartime role at Hendaye in 1940.
Salazar and Franco, the two Iberian dictators, signed the 1939 Iberian Pact and coordinated to keep the peninsula out of the Second World War.
Picasso became Franco's most famous artistic enemy, painting Guernica and the satirical 'Dream and Lie of Franco' and refusing to set foot in Spain while Franco ruled.
Theodora was Justinian's wife and effective co-ruler, famously steeling him to stay and crush the Nika riots instead of fleeing Constantinople.
Justinian entrusted Belisarius with the great campaigns to reconquer North Africa and Italy, alternately elevating and disgracing his most brilliant general.
Tchaikovsky and Dvorak formed a close friendship after meeting in 1888, each visiting the other's country to conduct concerts and exchanging warm correspondence.
Dvorak played viola for over a decade in the Provisional Theatre orchestra conducted by Smetana, absorbing his nationalist style and being encouraged by him as the next major figure in Czech music.
Hesse underwent Jungian analysis during his 1916 psychological crisis and met with Jung directly, an experience that shaped his novel Demian and his subsequent turn toward psychological themes.
Khrushchev rose through the party ranks as a loyal Stalinist enforcer, running purges in Ukraine and Moscow, before later denouncing Stalin's crimes in his 1956 Secret Speech.
Khrushchev led the post-Stalin power struggle against Beria, orchestrating his arrest in June 1953 and subsequent execution.
Molotov led the 1957 'Anti-Party Group' attempt to remove Khrushchev from power, which Khrushchev defeated, resulting in Molotov's expulsion from the leadership.
Zhukov's military backing was decisive in helping Khrushchev defeat the 1957 Anti-Party Group, though Khrushchev dismissed Zhukov from power later that same year fearing his influence.
Eisenhower and Khrushchev were opposing Cold War leaders whose 1959 Camp David summit thaw was wrecked by the 1960 U-2 spy plane incident, collapsing the planned Paris summit.
Mao and Khrushchev's ideological disputes over de-Stalinization and peaceful coexistence drove the Sino-Soviet split, rupturing relations between the two communist powers.
Charles V retroactively legitimized Cortes's unauthorized conquest of Mexico, naming him governor and later granting him the title Marques del Valle de Oaxaca.
Bohr worked in Rutherford's Manchester laboratory in 1912 and built his revolutionary quantum model of the atom directly on Rutherford's nuclear model.

During Schrodinger's 1926 visit to Copenhagen, Bohr relentlessly pressed him to accept the probabilistic Copenhagen interpretation over wave mechanics, a clash so intense it reportedly made the ill Schrodinger wish he had never discovered his equation.
Sikh tradition holds that Guru Nanak was captured during Babur's 1521 invasion and sack of Eminabad, an episode he mourned in his own hymns known as the Baburvani.

Alexander VI made his son Cesare a cardinal and then released him to become a secular prince, funding and politically backing his military conquest of the Romagna in the defining act of Borgia nepotism.
Alexander VI arranged Lucrezia's successive political marriages to the Sforza, Aragon, and Este families to forge and dissolve alliances as papal strategy required.

Alexander VI excommunicated Savonarola in 1497 for his defiant preaching against papal corruption, and after Savonarola's fall from Florentine favor he was tortured and executed with the pope's approval in 1498.
Rodrigo Borgia was elected Pope Alexander VI in 1492 immediately following the death of Innocent VIII.
Pius III was elected pope in 1503 immediately following Alexander VI's death.

According to the Book of Jeremiah, after Nebuchadnezzar's forces destroyed Jerusalem he specifically ordered his guard commander to locate Jeremiah, treat him well, and grant him freedom to remain in the land.

Mill corresponded extensively with Comte and organized financial subscriptions among English admirers to support him, before publicly breaking with Comte's later positivist system in his 1865 critique.
Ingres trained in David's Paris studio, where he absorbed the neoclassical discipline he would carry forward and eventually push beyond his teacher's style.
Ingres and Delacroix personified the defining artistic rivalry of 19th-century France, classical draftsmanship versus romantic color, and openly disparaged each other's work throughout their careers.
The young Degas met the elderly Ingres, whose advice to draw lines and work from memory shaped Degas's lifelong devotion to draftsmanship.
Napoleon's imperial administration commissioned official portraits from Ingres, including Napoleon on His Imperial Throne, launching his career as a state painter.

Within days of learning of Oersted's 1820 discovery that electric current deflects a compass needle, Ampere began corresponding with him and rapidly built the mathematical theory of electrodynamics on Oersted's finding.
Schrodinger succeeded Planck in his prestigious theoretical physics chair at the University of Berlin when Planck retired in 1927.

Planck personally appealed to Hitler in 1933 to spare Jewish scientists from dismissal, an intervention Hitler angrily rejected; Planck's own son Erwin was later executed by the Nazi regime for involvement in the 1944 plot against Hitler's life.
Ezekiel was among the Judean elite deported to Babylon by Nebuchadnezzar in 597 BCE, and his prophetic book directly addresses Nebuchadnezzar's conquests, including the siege of Jerusalem and Tyre.
Brigham Young succeeded Joseph Smith as leader of the Latter Day Saint movement after Smith's 1844 assassination, leading the church westward to Utah.
Galvani's claim of 'animal electricity' in frog legs sparked a famous scientific dispute with Volta, who insisted the effect was purely metallic and built the voltaic pile in 1800 to prove it.
Napoleon was so impressed by Volta's 1801 demonstration of the voltaic pile in Paris that he awarded him a gold medal and later made him a count and senator of the Kingdom of Italy.
Zetkin and Luxemburg were close political allies and personal friends on the revolutionary left of German social democracy, jointly opposing World War I and the party's reformist leadership.

Luxemburg was a fellow leading Marxist revolutionary who publicly clashed with Lenin over party organization and, in her essay on the Russian Revolution, criticized Bolshevik suppression of democracy and civil liberties.
Mark was Barnabas's cousin, and their kinship led Barnabas to take Mark on missionary work even after Paul rejected him, causing Barnabas and Paul to part ways as recorded in Acts 15.
After Mark's early desertion caused a rift severe enough to split Paul and Barnabas's missionary partnership, Paul later reconciled with him, calling him useful for ministry in his final epistle.

Basil II, who never married, kept his niece Zoe unmarried and out of the succession for decades, only arranging her marriage in 1028 as he lay dying without an heir, leaving her to determine the dynasty's future.
Antony's funeral oration for Caesar turned the Roman mob against Brutus and the other assassins, and Antony then led the armies that crushed Brutus at the Battle of Philippi in 42 BCE.
Octavian joined the Second Triumvirate to avenge his adoptive father Caesar's murder, commanding forces alongside Antony that defeated and killed Brutus at Philippi in 42 BCE.
Riemann studied under Dirichlet in Berlin and considered him, after Gauss, his greatest mathematical influence; Riemann later succeeded Dirichlet's chair at Gottingen upon his death in 1859.
Childhood friends from Aix-en-Provence, their decades-long bond ended in 1886 when Zola published L'Oeuvre, a novel widely read as portraying Cezanne as a failed, tortured artist.
Cezanne exhibited alongside Monet in the pioneering Impressionist group shows of the 1870s, and Monet later hosted him and organized purchases of his work to support him.
Gauguin collected and openly studied Cezanne's early paintings, but Cezanne grew resentful, feeling Gauguin had appropriated his pictorial innovations for his own developing style.
Edison and Bell competed directly in telephone technology, with Edison's carbon microphone transmitter improving on and challenging patents tied to Bell's original telephone design.

Bell examined the young deaf-blind Keller and connected her family with the Perkins Institute, leading to the arrival of teacher Anne Sullivan; he remained a lifelong friend and mentor, and Keller dedicated her autobiography to him.
Half-brothers locked in a bitter power struggle, Sigismund had Wenceslaus captured and imprisoned for over a year in 1402 before eventually succeeding him as King of Bohemia after his death in 1419.

Sigismund guaranteed Hus safe conduct to the Council of Constance in 1414 but then allowed the Council to try him for heresy and burn him at the stake in 1415, a betrayal that ignited the Hussite Wars.
Chanel and Churchill became friends through shared hunting parties in Scotland hosted by her lover the Duke of Westminster, a friendship that endured for decades.
Chanel and Picasso were close friends within the Parisian avant-garde circle around the Ballets Russes, where she contributed costume design work alongside his set and costume commissions.
Chanel housed the exiled Stravinsky and his family at her villa Bel Respiro in 1920-21 while they carried on an affair, and she anonymously financed the celebrated 1920 revival of his Rite of Spring.
Chanel designed the costumes for several of Cocteau's theatrical productions, including his 1922 staging of Antigone and 1926 Orphee.
Mary's fierce lifelong Catholicism and refusal to accept her own illegitimacy were rooted in defending her mother Catherine's marriage against Henry VIII's annulment.
Henry VIII declared his daughter Mary illegitimate after annulling his marriage to Catherine of Aragon, banishing her from court for years before eventually restoring her to the line of succession.
Mary married Philip of Spain in 1554 to forge a Catholic alliance, a deeply unpopular match in Protestant-leaning England that sparked rebellion and left her without an heir.
Mary's Protestant half-brother Edward VI, dying without an heir, rewrote the succession in his will to exclude her in favor of Lady Jane Grey, precipitating the succession crisis Mary won by force.
Henry IV's assassination in 1610 put his eight-year-old son Louis XIII on the throne of France under his mother's regency.

Marie de' Medici ruled as regent for her young son Louis XIII, but their relationship collapsed at the 1630 Day of the Dupes when Louis sided with Richelieu against her, leading to her permanent exile.

Louis XIII backed Richelieu as his chief minister for eighteen years, repeatedly protecting him from court conspiracies including his own mother's, enabling Richelieu to centralize French royal power.
Alexandra became deeply dependent on Rasputin as a spiritual healer for her hemophiliac son Alexei, a trust he exploited to gain political influence that scandalized the Russian court.
Degas was an early admirer and collector of Gauguin's work, encouraging him during his transition from stockbroker to painter and supporting his participation in the Impressionist exhibitions.
Durkheim and Bergson were rival intellectual authorities in French academia, with Durkheim's positivist sociology directly contesting Bergson's vitalist philosophy of knowledge and religion.
Selim forced his father Bayezid II to abdicate the Ottoman throne in 1512 after a military showdown, and Bayezid died mysteriously weeks later while traveling into exile.
Selim I crushed Shah Ismail I's Safavid army at the Battle of Chaldiran in 1514, a decisive Sunni-Shia confrontation that checked Safavid expansion into Anatolia.
Suleiman the Magnificent succeeded his father Selim I as Ottoman Sultan upon his death in 1520, inheriting a greatly expanded empire.
Mann and Hesse maintained a close decades-long friendship and correspondence as fellow German exile writers, each championing the other's work, both eventually winning the Nobel Prize in Literature.
Rolland and Hesse formed a close friendship as fellow pacifist writers during World War I, corresponding and meeting in Switzerland to support each other's antiwar stances.
Josephine was the maternal grandmother of Napoleon III through her daughter Hortense de Beauharnais, linking her bloodline to the second French Empire despite her divorce from Napoleon I.
While Paracelsus served as town physician in Basel in the late 1520s, he treated Erasmus for chronic ailments and the two corresponded, with Erasmus praising his medical knowledge.
During his travels through Anatolia in the 1330s, Ibn Battuta was received and hosted by Orhan, whom he described in his Rihla as one of the greatest rulers of the Turkmen.

Despite earlier backing Italian unification against Austria, Napoleon III kept French troops garrisoned in Rome to protect the Pope, and they crushed Garibaldi's redshirts at the 1867 Battle of Mentana when he tried to seize the city.

Britain and France fought as allies against Russia in the Crimean War, and Victoria and Napoleon III exchanged warm state visits to Windsor and Paris that cemented an unusually close Anglo-French friendship.

Under the secret Plombieres Agreement of 1858, Napoleon III allied with Victor Emmanuel II against Austria in the 1859 war that enabled Italian unification, in exchange for ceding Nice and Savoy to France.

Napoleon III installed Maximilian as Emperor of Mexico during the French intervention, backing him with French troops, then withdrew that support under US pressure, leaving Maximilian to be captured and executed in 1867.
Urban VII was elected pope in September 1590 following the death of Sixtus V that August.
Gregory XIV was elected pope in December 1590 after a prolonged conclave following Urban VII's death just twelve days into his own pontificate.
Nansen, Norway's pioneering polar explorer, mentored the younger Amundsen and lent him his ship the Fram for the expedition that reached the South Pole in 1911.

Scott and Amundsen raced to be first to the South Pole in 1911-12; Amundsen's team arrived first and returned safely, while Scott's party reached the pole a month later and died on the return journey.
In 1920s Paris, Pound closely edited the young Hemingway's prose, teaching him to cut adjectives and tighten his sentences, a lesson Hemingway credited with shaping his signature style.

Fitzgerald befriended Hemingway in Paris, championed his early work to his own editor Max Perkins at Scribner's, and helped shape The Sun Also Rises, though their friendship later soured into mutual resentment.
Berlioz and Liszt were close friends and mutual champions of Romantic innovation, with Liszt conducting premieres of Berlioz's works in Weimar after they faced hostile reception in Paris.
Schumann and Liszt exchanged mutual dedications, Schumann's Fantasie in C major to Liszt and Liszt's Sonata in B minor to Schumann, marking a warm if later complicated friendship between the two.

After Robert Schumann's decline, Clara became a leading voice of the conservative musical camp that viewed Liszt's progressive New German School with hostility, a divide that defined the era's 'War of the Romantics.'
Otto I had his son Otto II crowned co-emperor in 967 to secure the succession, and Otto II became sole Holy Roman Emperor upon his father's death in 973.
Clement VII allied with Francis I in the League of Cognac against Charles V, and in 1533 personally traveled to Marseille to officiate the marriage uniting the Medici and French royal houses.

Clement VII, head of the Medici family, personally arranged and presided over his kinswoman Catherine's 1533 marriage to Henry of France, a union intended to secure Medici and papal influence in France.

Ivan corresponded extensively with Elizabeth I to build an English trade alliance through the Muscovy Company, even proposing marriage and requesting a promise of asylum in England should he need to flee Russia.

Husserl mentored Heidegger in phenomenology and secured him his Freiburg professorship, but Heidegger later removed the dedication of Being and Time to him and enforced Nazi racial policies that excluded the Jewish Husserl from the university.

Jaspers and Heidegger were close intellectual friends and correspondents through the 1920s, but the friendship collapsed after Heidegger's 1933 embrace of Nazism, given Jaspers's Jewish wife, and the two barely reconciled after the war.
In his political testament before his suicide in April 1945, Hitler named Donitz as his successor as head of state, making him the last leader of Nazi Germany until its surrender weeks later.

Donatello, Masaccio, and Brunelleschi formed the innovating trio of early Florentine Renaissance art, jointly developing the principles of naturalism and perspective that Vasari credited them with pioneering together.
Augustus personally supported Horace, offered him a position as his secretary which the poet declined, and commissioned him to compose the Carmen Saeculare for the Secular Games of 17 BCE.
Pius V organized the Holy League with Philip II and Venice against the Ottoman Empire, an alliance that achieved the decisive naval victory at the Battle of Lepanto in 1571.

Hurrem is widely held to have conspired with Rustem Pasha to convince Suleiman that his popular eldest son Mustafa, born to a rival consort, was plotting treason, leading to Suleiman ordering his strangulation in 1553 to secure succession for her own sons.
Hurrem and Suleiman's powerful Grand Vizier Ibrahim Pasha competed for the sultan's favor at court, a rivalry historians link to Ibrahim's sudden execution in 1536.
Gregory XVI was elected pope in 1831 following the death of Pius VIII the previous year.

Gregory XVI relied on Metternich's Austria to send troops that crushed the 1831 liberal uprisings in the Papal States, part of their shared conservative front against revolutionary movements in Italy.
Hulagu was Genghis Khan's grandson through Tolui, inheriting command of the western Mongol armies that he used to found the Ilkhanate in Persia and the Middle East.
Great Khan Mongke personally dispatched his brother Hulagu on the westward campaign that destroyed the Abbasid Caliphate at Baghdad in 1258 and established the Ilkhanate.
During the Toluid Civil War following Mongke's death, Hulagu backed his brother Kublai's claim to be Great Khan over their brother Ariq Boke, helping legitimize Kublai's rule.
Murad II succeeded his father Mehmed I as Ottoman Sultan upon his death in 1421.
Skanderbeg led a prolonged Albanian revolt against Ottoman rule beginning in 1443, repeatedly defeating Murad II's forces and personally repelling the sultan's own siege of Kruje in 1450.

Columbus was reportedly present at the Portuguese court in December 1488 when Dias returned from rounding the Cape of Good Hope, a triumph that helped doom Columbus's westward proposal there and pushed him to seek Spanish backing instead.
Dias commanded a ship in Cabral's 1500 fleet to India, the voyage that incidentally discovered Brazil, and died when a storm sank four of the fleet's ships off the Cape of Good Hope.
Louis XVIII fled France as his older brother Louis XVI was arrested and executed during the Revolution, spending decades in exile before eventually reclaiming the throne their family had lost.
Charles X succeeded his older brother Louis XVIII as King of France upon his death in 1824.

Louis XVIII was restored to the French throne after Napoleon's 1814 abdication, only to flee again when Napoleon returned during the Hundred Days, before reclaiming his throne for good after Waterloo.
Louis XVIII recognized his young imprisoned nephew as the rightful King Louis XVII from exile, only assuming the royal title and numbering himself after the boy's death in captivity in 1795.

Crassus served under Sulla in the civil war, playing a key role in the decisive Battle of the Colline Gate in 82 BCE, and built the foundation of his enormous fortune buying up properties confiscated in Sulla's proscriptions.
Herod Agrippa, raised alongside Claudius in Rome, personally negotiated with the Senate on his behalf after Caligula's assassination in 41, helping secure his acclamation as emperor.
Claudius gave Vespasian command of the Second Augusta Legion during the 43 CE invasion of Britain, the campaign that launched his military reputation and eventual path to the imperial throne.
After the Decembrist uprising, Nicholas I personally appointed himself Pushkin's sole censor, granting him certain freedoms denied other writers while tightly controlling what he could publish.
Pushkin befriended the younger Gogol and is credited with giving him the original plot ideas for both The Government Inspector and Dead Souls.
Johann Strauss I opposed his son's musical ambitions and competed with him as a rival orchestra leader in Vienna, but the younger Strauss ultimately eclipsed his father's fame as the 'Waltz King.'
Strauss and Brahms were close friends and mutual admirers in Vienna; Brahms famously signed a fan's autograph book with a few bars of The Blue Danube captioned 'Unfortunately, not by Brahms.'
Offenbach met Strauss in Vienna and encouraged him to try composing for the stage, advice that led Strauss toward the operettas, including Die Fledermaus, that became central to his legacy.
Philip II continued his father Charles V's patronage of Titian, commissioning the celebrated 'poesie' mythological paintings that occupied the artist for decades of his later career.

Mussolini's government initially funded and promoted Montessori's method as a point of national pride, but she refused to force fascist uniforms and salutes on her students, prompting Mussolini to shut down all Montessori schools in Italy in 1934 and drive her into exile.

Gandhi visited Montessori's teacher training course in London in 1931 and expressed deep admiration for her method, forming a mutual regard rooted in their shared commitment to child-centered, humanitarian education.
Hadrian adopted Antoninus Pius as his heir in 138 CE shortly before his death, on the condition that Antoninus in turn adopt Marcus Aurelius, securing the succession for decades to come.

Rodin visited the 1902 Vienna Secession exhibition to see Klimt's Beethoven Frieze displayed alongside his own sculptures, a visit Klimt described as deeply moving validation from a master he revered.

Robert and Clara married in 1840 after a legal battle to overcome her father's fierce opposition, forming one of the era's great musical partnerships until Robert's institutionalization and death in the mid-1850s.

Mendelssohn and Schumann were close friends and colleagues in Leipzig's musical life, with Mendelssohn conducting premieres of Schumann's works and both shaping the city's conservatory and concert scene together.

As Cardinal Giuliano della Rovere, the future Julius II was Alexander VI's fiercest rival and fled Rome fearing for his life during the Borgia pope's reign, later working to dismantle Borgia power once he himself became pope.
Julius II appointed Bramante as chief architect for his ambitious rebuilding of St. Peter's Basilica, begun in 1506, the most consequential architectural commission of the High Renaissance.

After allying with Louis XII against Venice in the League of Cambrai, Julius II reversed course and formed the Holy League to drive French forces out of Italy, famously declaring his intent to expel the 'barbarians.'
Giovanni de' Medici was elected Pope Leo X in 1513 immediately following Julius II's death.
Einstein visited the elderly Michelson in California in 1931, months before his death, paying tribute to the precision experiments whose null result had helped clear the way for special relativity.
Zeno was Parmenides's most famous student, devising his celebrated paradoxes of motion specifically to defend his teacher's monist doctrine that reality is a single, unchanging whole.
Aristophanes repeatedly parodied Euripides as a clever but morally suspect innovator in comedies including Acharnians, Thesmophoriazusae, and Frogs, making him a recurring target of his comic satire.

Plato, a younger contemporary in Athens's small literary circle, featured Aristophanes as a central and sympathetically drawn character in his Symposium, giving him the famous speech on the mythical origin of love.

Kierkegaard's first published work, a 1838 review of Andersen's novel Only a Fiddler, sharply criticized Andersen for lacking a coherent life-view, launching his career with a direct literary attack on his fellow Copenhagen writer.
Gregory XIII was elected pope in 1572 following the death of Pius V.
Sixtus V was elected pope in 1585 following the death of Gregory XIII.

Gregory XIII celebrated Catherine de' Medici's orchestration of the 1572 St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre of Huguenots with a Te Deum mass and a commemorative medal, hailing it as a victory for the Catholic cause.
Gregory XIII backed Philip II's Counter-Reformation efforts, including support for plots against Elizabeth I of England and the suppression of the Protestant revolt in the Netherlands.
Woolf visited the elderly Hardy at Max Gate in 1926, recording the meeting in her diary as a memorable encounter with a writer she deeply admired.
Baudelaire and Manet were close companions in the Parisian avant-garde circle, and Manet painted portraits of Baudelaire and his mistress Jeanne Duval.

As a reader for the NRF publishing house, Gide famously rejected the manuscript of Swann's Way in 1912, one of literary history's most regretted decisions, and later apologized profusely as the two became friends and mutual admirers.
Proust and Cocteau moved in the same elite Parisian literary salons, with the younger Cocteau becoming a friend and early champion who helped promote Proust's work among the avant-garde.

Under pressure from their grandmother Julia Maesa, Elagabalus adopted his cousin Severus Alexander as heir, then grew jealous and reportedly plotted against him before being assassinated by the Praetorian Guard in 222, who then proclaimed Alexander emperor.
Paganini and Rossini were friends and fellow Italian musical celebrities who moved in the same circles in Italy and Paris, each the most famous figure of his respective musical domain.

Deeply moved after hearing Harold in Italy, the viola concerto he had commissioned but declined to perform himself, Paganini gave Berlioz a gift of 20,000 francs in 1838, declaring him Beethoven's true successor.

Catherine de' Medici orchestrated the St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre during the Paris wedding celebrations of her daughter Marguerite to the Protestant Henry, forcing him to convert to Catholicism to survive and holding him a virtual prisoner at court for years afterward.
Philip II backed the Catholic League against Henry IV in the French Wars of Religion, sending Spanish troops to help besiege Paris and pushing his own daughter's claim to the French throne.
Elizabeth I sent troops and subsidies to support the Protestant Henry IV against the Catholic League and Spain during the French Wars of Religion.
Henry IV married Marie de' Medici in 1600 after his first marriage was annulled, and she bore him his heir, the future Louis XIII.
Becquerel's discovery of radioactivity in uranium salts directly launched the Curies' research program, and the three shared the 1903 Nobel Prize in Physics for their combined work on the phenomenon.
Pierre Curie's daughter Irene, only nine when he died in 1906, went on to continue the family's radioactivity research and win her own Nobel Prize in Chemistry.
Ali became the fourth Rashidun Caliph after Uthman was assassinated by rebels in 656, a succession clouded by controversy that helped ignite the First Fitna civil war.
Leo XI was elected pope in April 1605 following the death of Clement VIII the previous month.
Paul V was elected pope in May 1605 after Leo XI died just twenty-seven days into his own pontificate.

As papal legate to France, the future Leo XI personally received Henry IV's abjuration of Protestantism in 1595, and his reputation as a friend of France helped secure his own election as pope a decade later.

Diderot and d'Alembert co-edited the Encyclopedie for years, the monumental Enlightenment project that d'Alembert eventually abandoned under censorship pressure, leaving Diderot to see it through alone.
As prefect of Isere, Fourier recognized the young Ampere's mathematical talent in Grenoble and encouraged his early scientific career.
Ampere's action-at-distance theory of electromagnetism and Faraday's field-based approach put the two scientists in direct scientific competition, spurring Faraday's 1821 rotation experiments.
As Valide Sultan, Nurbanu wielded major political influence over the Ottoman court during the reign of her son Murad III.
Mehmed III succeeded his father Murad III as Ottoman Sultan in 1595.
Elizabeth I and Murad III exchanged letters establishing an Anglo-Ottoman trade and diplomatic alliance, partly aimed against Habsburg Spain.
Pius VII was elected pope in 1800 following the death of Pius VI, who had died in French captivity.
Leo XII was elected pope in 1823 following the death of Pius VII.
El Greco trained in Titian's workshop in Venice, absorbing the Venetian colorist technique that shaped his mature style.
Philip II commissioned El Greco to paint works for El Escorial, including The Martyrdom of St. Maurice, though the king's dissatisfaction with the result ended hopes of further royal patronage.
In Rome, El Greco publicly criticized Michelangelo's Last Judgment and offered to repaint it more decently, a boast that scandalized Roman art circles and damaged his own reputation there.
King Wenceslaus IV issued the 1409 Kutna Hora Decree giving Czech masters control of Prague University, cementing Hus's position and protecting the reform movement for years.
Zola publicly defended Manet's controversial paintings in print, and Manet repaid the friendship by painting a celebrated portrait of Zola in 1868.
Zola was a regular guest at Flaubert's Paris salon alongside fellow realist and naturalist writers, and the two maintained a close literary friendship until Flaubert's death.
Maupassant was the standout contributor to Les Soirees de Medan, the 1880 naturalist anthology organized around Zola, who served as the movement's leading figure and guide for younger writers.
Ahmed I succeeded his father Mehmed III as Ottoman Sultan in 1603.
Upon Ahmed I's death in 1617, his brother Mustafa I succeeded him as Ottoman Sultan, the first time the throne passed to the eldest surviving male of the dynasty rather than a son.
Kosem Sultan was the favorite consort and legal wife of Ahmed I, and mother of two future sultans, Murad IV and Ibrahim.

Einstein and Schrodinger exchanged extensive correspondence probing the interpretation of quantum mechanics, an exchange that directly led Schrodinger to devise the Schrodinger's cat thought experiment in response to Einstein's EPR paper.
Schrodinger built his wave equation directly on de Broglie's matter-wave hypothesis, and the two physicists remained aligned in advocating a wave-based, realist interpretation of quantum mechanics.
According to Christian tradition, Barbara was martyred in 306 during the Diocletianic persecution of Christians for refusing to renounce her faith.
Mark Antony backed Herod's appointment as King of Judea by the Roman Senate in 40 BC, establishing his rule.
After Actium, Augustus confirmed Herod as client king of Judea and expanded his territory, backing his rule for the rest of his reign.
According to the Gospel of Matthew, Herod ordered the Massacre of the Innocents in Bethlehem in an attempt to kill the infant Jesus, whom he saw as a threat to his throne.

Herod the Great executed Agrippa's father Aristobulus IV on suspicion of conspiracy, orphaning the young Agrippa and sending him to be raised in Rome, a family tragedy that shaped Agrippa's later path to the throne his grandfather once held.
Lorenzo de' Medici groomed his son Giovanni for a church career from childhood and secured his cardinalate at age 13, laying the groundwork for his eventual election as Pope Leo X.
Leo X, a fellow Medici, commissioned Michelangelo for major Medici family projects including the facade of San Lorenzo in Florence.
Leo X was Raphael's chief patron in Rome, commissioning the Vatican tapestry cartoons and naming him chief architect of St. Peter's Basilica.
In the 1521 Treaty of Worms, Leo X allied with Charles V against France in the Italian Wars, an alliance sealed shortly before Leo's death that year.
After negotiating the 1516 Concordat of Bologna with Francis I, Leo X reversed course and allied with Charles V against France in the Italian Wars, contesting French control of Milan.
Adrian VI was elected pope in 1522 following the death of Leo X.
Montgomery's Eighth Army defeated Rommel's Afrika Korps at El Alamein in 1942, making the two commanders the defining rival generals of the North African campaign.
Montgomery served as a top Allied ground commander under Supreme Allied Commander Eisenhower during the Normandy invasion and subsequent campaign in Europe, despite frequent friction over strategy.
Churchill appointed Montgomery to command the Eighth Army in North Africa in 1942, a decision that launched his rise to Britain's most prominent wartime general.
Pius VIII was elected pope in 1829 following the death of Leo XII.
Peter and John were paired repeatedly in the early church's ministry, jointly healing a lame man at the Temple and being sent together to confirm converts in Samaria.
D'Alembert corresponded with and encouraged the young Lagrange and helped recommend him for the post at the Berlin Academy of Sciences.
Frederick the Great invited Lagrange to Berlin to succeed Euler as director of mathematics at the Prussian Academy of Sciences, where he worked for over twenty years.
Napoleon honored Lagrange with a senatorship and made him a Count of the Empire, recognizing him as one of France's leading scientific figures.

As the two leading figures of the French Academy of Sciences, Lagrange and Laplace worked alongside each other for decades on celestial mechanics and mathematical physics, examining and building on each other's results.
Kandinsky and Klee were close friends and colleagues who taught together at the Bauhaus and later formed the exhibiting group Die Blaue Vier (The Blue Four).
Sixtus IV backed the 1478 Pazzi Conspiracy against Lorenzo, an assassination plot that killed his brother Giuliano and led to war between the Papal States and Florence.

Savonarola's fiery sermons condemning Medici excess and corruption made him Lorenzo's chief moral antagonist in Florence in the final years of his rule, and tradition holds Savonarola was summoned to Lorenzo's deathbed.
Cook's three Pacific voyages of exploration were commissioned and funded by the Royal Navy under George III, who took a personal interest in their scientific and geographic discoveries.
Flaubert, a friend of Maupassant's mother, took the young writer under his wing for years, rigorously critiquing his early drafts and introducing him to literary Paris, including Zola's circle.
Ancient sources report that Socrates rarely attended the theater except to see Euripides's plays and was rumored to have collaborated with him on his tragedies, reflecting a documented mutual regard.
The two greatest poets of the Tang dynasty met in 744, traveled together, and exchanged poems of mutual admiration that survive as some of the most celebrated friendship poetry in Chinese literature.
Matthias was chosen by lot to replace Judas Iscariot as the twelfth apostle after Judas's betrayal and death, as recounted in Acts 1.
According to Acts, Matthias had accompanied Jesus and his followers throughout his entire ministry, from John's baptism to the ascension, qualifying him to be chosen as a replacement apostle.
Manet and Degas were close but prickly friends in the Paris avant-garde; Manet once slashed apart a double portrait Degas had painted of him and his wife because he disliked how she was depicted.
Mirbeau was one of Rodin's earliest and most vocal champions in the press, writing influential articles defending his sculpture against public and critical hostility.
Mirbeau was a close friend and neighbor of Monet at Giverny and one of the most influential critical defenders of Impressionism in the French press.
Mirbeau was one of the earliest and fiercest defenders of Zola's J'Accuse and the cause of Alfred Dreyfus, aligning with him publicly during the Dreyfus Affair.
As leader of the Jerusalem church, James met with Paul and extended him the right hand of fellowship at the Council of Jerusalem, formally endorsing his mission to the Gentiles.
James and Peter were the two central leaders of the early Jerusalem church, jointly shaping its direction, including the resolution of the dispute over requirements for Gentile converts.
Titus succeeded his father Vespasian as Roman emperor in 79 AD, having already served as his designated heir and military commander.
Domitian succeeded his brother Titus as Roman emperor in 81 AD after Titus's sudden death, amid persistent rumors that Domitian had a hand in it.
Josephus served Titus as an interpreter and advisor during the siege of Jerusalem, and Titus later brought him to Rome under Flavian patronage where he wrote his histories.
According to Christian tradition, Lucy was martyred in 304 during the Diocletianic persecution of Christians for refusing to renounce her faith and give up her virginity.
According to Christian tradition, Catherine was martyred around 305 for publicly denouncing Diocletian's persecution of Christians and refusing to renounce her faith.

Hatshepsut ruled as senior co-regent and pharaoh while her stepson Thutmose III was a minor; after her death he became sole pharaoh and later ordered her images and cartouches systematically erased from monuments.

Goring was one of Hitler's earliest and most loyal supporters from the 1923 Beer Hall Putsch onward, rose to command the Luftwaffe, and was named Hitler's official successor until their final falling-out in 1945.
Goring openly despised Ribbentrop, mocking his diplomatic judgment, and the two competed bitterly for Hitler's favor and influence over German foreign and military policy.
Marshall recognized Eisenhower's talent early, jumped him over dozens of more senior officers, and appointed him Supreme Allied Commander for the invasion of Europe.
Roosevelt appointed Marshall Army Chief of Staff and relied on him as his principal military strategist throughout World War II, an exceptionally close working partnership.
Truman appointed Marshall Secretary of State and later Secretary of Defense, relying on him to design the Marshall Plan and shape early Cold War policy.
Mary became Queen of Scots at just six days old upon the death of her father James V in 1542.

Saul, later Paul, gave approval to and guarded the cloaks of those who stoned Stephen to death, the first Christian martyr, an event Acts frames as the backdrop to Paul's own persecution of the early church.

Elizabeth was the wife of the priest Zechariah, and their unexpected conception of John the Baptist in old age, announced to Zechariah by the angel Gabriel, is a central episode in the Gospel of Luke.
Thomson supervised Rutherford's early research at the Cavendish Laboratory, and Rutherford went on to succeed him as the laboratory's director in 1919.
Bohr came to work under Thomson at the Cavendish Laboratory in 1911, though the strained fit led him to move to Rutherford's lab in Manchester, where he developed his atomic model.
Wilson developed the cloud chamber while working under Thomson's direction at the Cavendish Laboratory, a device that became essential to experimental particle physics.
Antony, Lepidus, and Octavian formed the Second Triumvirate in 43 BCE, jointly ruling Rome and conducting the proscriptions before Lepidus was sidelined from power.

Wilde befriended the young Gide in Paris and North Africa in the early 1890s, and their conversations profoundly shook Gide's sense of morality and creative freedom, an influence Gide later chronicled in his memoirs.
Henry II married Catherine de' Medici in 1533, though he favored his mistress Diane de Poitiers throughout the marriage; Catherine became regent for their sons after his death.
Catherine ruled as regent and the dominant political power behind her son Charles IX for most of his reign, including during the St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre.
Catherine was her favorite son Henry III's closest political advisor and worked to secure his elections first as King of Poland and then of France.
Twain befriended Helen Keller as a young woman, calling her one of the most remarkable people of the age, and helped arrange the funding that allowed her to attend Radcliffe College.
Ibsen and Bjornson were Norway's two towering literary figures of the era, moving between close friendship and bitter rivalry over politics, theater, and public reputation throughout their careers.

Strindberg waged a one-sided literary feud against Ibsen for years, attacking his plays in print, while Ibsen reportedly kept a portrait of Strindberg on his wall to keep himself provoked while writing.
Ibsen commissioned Grieg to compose the incidental music for Peer Gynt, resulting in one of the most famous orchestral scores ever written for a play.
Clement VII was elected pope in 1523 following the death of Adrian VI.
Adrian tutored the young Charles V in the Netherlands and later served as his regent in Spain, a relationship of trust that helped secure Adrian's own election as pope.

As pope, Adrian VI confronted the spreading Lutheran Reformation, sending envoys to the 1522 Diet of Nuremberg who candidly admitted Church corruption while still demanding action against Luther's teachings.
Nero appointed Vespasian to command the Roman legions suppressing the Jewish revolt in 67 AD, a command that became the springboard for Vespasian's own bid for the throne after Nero's death.
Vespasian spared the captured Jewish commander Josephus after Josephus predicted he would become emperor, and once the prophecy came true, Vespasian freed him and became his lifelong patron in Rome.
Domitian was Vespasian's younger son, and though Vespasian favored his elder son Titus with real power, Domitian eventually became the third and final Flavian emperor.
Joule and Thomson collaborated on experiments establishing an absolute temperature scale, discovering the Joule-Thomson effect used in modern refrigeration.
Faraday recognized the significance of Joule's early experiments on the mechanical equivalent of heat when other scientists dismissed them, and helped present his work to the Royal Society.
Rossini visited the deaf and ailing Beethoven in Vienna in 1822, a meeting of the era's two most famous living composers that Rossini recounted vividly for the rest of his life.
Rossini and Donizetti were the leading rivals of early 19th-century Italian opera, competing for the same audiences and opera houses across Italy before Rossini's early retirement from composing.

Wagner visited the elderly Rossini in Paris in 1860, and their long conversation, later documented by Edmond Michotte, revealed unexpected mutual respect between two composers often seen as opposites.
Xavier was one of Ignatius's original companions at the University of Paris and co-founded the Society of Jesus with him in 1540, later becoming its greatest missionary.
Pope Paul III formally approved the founding of the Society of Jesus in 1540, giving Ignatius's new order papal sanction and setting it on course to become a central force of the Counter-Reformation.
Pope Callixtus III named Skanderbeg Captain General of the Holy See and sent him financial and material support for his resistance against the Ottomans.
Pope Pius II championed Skanderbeg's cause and was organizing a crusade to relieve Albania from Ottoman pressure when he died in 1464 at Ancona awaiting the fleet's departure.

Stendhal served as an official in Napoleon's military administration, including during the Russian campaign, and his lifelong admiration for Napoleon shaped his fiction's fascination with ambition and energy.
Balzac wrote a lengthy, glowing public review praising Stendhal's The Charterhouse of Parma, a rare and significant endorsement between two major French novelists of the era.
Queen Mary was George VI's mother, and after Edward VIII's abdication she gave her second son strong public and private support as he took the throne unexpectedly.

Bishop Ambrose of Milan excommunicated Theodosius and forced him to perform public penance after the emperor's troops massacred thousands of civilians at Thessalonica in 390, a landmark assertion of Church authority over the state.
Theodosius was the father of Honorius and, upon his death in 395, split the empire between his two sons, with Honorius receiving the Western Roman Empire.

After conquering Al-Biruni's homeland of Khwarazm, Mahmud of Ghazni brought him to his court and later took him to India, where his patronage, however coercive, enabled Al-Biruni's landmark research into Indian science and culture.
Aethelwulf succeeded his father Ecgberht as King of Wessex in 839, continuing the expansion of West Saxon power that his father had begun.

Despite suffering from leprosy, Baldwin personally led the Crusader army that decisively defeated Saladin at the Battle of Montgisard in 1177, and the two remained the central opposing figures of the Kingdom of Jerusalem's wars until Baldwin's death.
Balian of Ibelin was a trusted baron in Baldwin IV's court and military campaigns, later leading the defense and negotiated surrender of Jerusalem to Saladin in 1187.
Nerva was proclaimed emperor by the Senate immediately after Domitian's assassination in 96 AD, ending the Flavian dynasty and beginning the era of the adoptive emperors.
Christian tradition holds that Domitian's persecution of Christians led to John's exile to the island of Patmos, where he is said to have written the Book of Revelation.
Constantine and his sons wrote to Anthony seeking his counsel, treating the desert hermit as a spiritual authority, though Anthony largely declined the emperor's invitation to visit Constantinople.

Richelieu negotiated the 1631 Treaty of Barwalde, subsidizing Gustavus Adolphus's Swedish campaign against the Habsburgs in the Thirty Years' War even though Catholic France was backing a Protestant army.
Christina inherited the Swedish throne at age six after her father Gustavus Adolphus was killed at the Battle of Lutzen in 1632.
Urban VIII was elected pope in 1623 following the death of Gregory XV.
Innocent X was elected pope in 1644 following the death of Urban VIII, and moved quickly to investigate the Barberini family's finances.
Urban VIII was Bernini's greatest patron, commissioning the bronze Baldachin over St. Peter's tomb and numerous other major works throughout his pontificate.

Mary Magdalene was one of Jesus's closest followers, present at the crucifixion and, according to all four Gospels, the first witness to his resurrection, earning her the traditional title 'apostle to the apostles'.
As a young man, Pericles served as choregos, the wealthy sponsor funding the production, for Aeschylus's play The Persians in 472 BCE, one of his first public roles in Athenian civic life.

Montaigne, serving as mayor of Bordeaux and a moderate Catholic during the Wars of Religion, met with and advised the Protestant Henry of Navarre, who later became Henry IV and cited Montaigne's counsel.
Pope Innocent VIII held Bayezid's rival brother Cem Sultan in custody and received a substantial annual payment from Bayezid to keep him away from the Ottoman throne.

Pope Alexander VI continued holding Bayezid's brother Cem Sultan hostage in exchange for large payments, and Bayezid is reported to have paid Alexander to have Cem poisoned before handing him to Charles VIII of France.
Appleton conducted his early radio research at the Cavendish Laboratory during Rutherford's tenure as director, part of the generation of physicists Rutherford shaped there.

Hasan and Husayn were brothers, sons of Ali and grandsons of Muhammad, both revered as the second and third Shia Imams; Hasan's earlier abdication of the caliphate to Muawiyah set the stage for Husayn's later stand at Karbala.

Muawiyah's efforts to secure the succession of his son Yazid as caliph, in violation of his treaty with Hasan, put him in direct political tension with Husayn, who refused to recognize the arrangement and would later be killed resisting it at Karbala.
Bayezid succeeded his father Murad I as Ottoman Sultan on the battlefield at Kosovo in 1389, immediately after Murad was assassinated following the Ottoman victory.
After Bayezid I's death in Timurid captivity, his son Mehmed I emerged victorious from the decade-long Ottoman Interregnum among his brothers to reunify and restore the Ottoman state.
Bayezid crushed Sigismund's Crusader army at the Battle of Nicopolis in 1396, one of the largest Crusades of the late Middle Ages and a defining defeat of Sigismund's early reign.
French forces under Napoleon's directive invaded the Papal States, and Pius VI was taken prisoner to France in 1799, where he died in captivity within weeks.

Pius VI traveled personally to Vienna in 1782 in an unprecedented attempt to persuade Joseph II to reverse his Josephinist reforms curbing Church authority, but the emperor's policies continued largely unchanged.
Pius VI was elected pope in 1775 following the death of Clement XIV.
